title: Phonon-bottlenecked spin relaxation of Er$^{3+}$:CaWO$_4$ at milliKelvin temperatures
authors: S. Rajendran, B. Mistri, P. K. Sharma, S. E. Kubatkin, A. V. Danilov, S. Dhomkar, S. E. de Graaf, V. Ranjan
abstract: We study spin-lattice relaxation times of electron spins in Er$^{3+}$:CaWO$_4$ at millikelvin temperature, detected via their coupling to a low-mode volume superconducting resonator. At large magnetic field supporting strong phonon-emission rates, we observe a noticeable increase in relaxation times with increasing spin-excitations, which exhibit a unique $[\tanh (\hbar ω_0/k_\text{B} T)]^2$ temperature dependence. These observations are typical of a phonon-bottlenecked spin relaxation, and have implications for quantum technologies that exploit rare-earth spin ensembles as coherent resources.
summary: The paper investigates how spins in Er3+:CaWO4 relax at millikelvin temperatures, discovering that a phonon bottleneck prevents efficient energy transfer to the thermal bath. This effect leads to unique temperature and magnetic field dependencies that are critical to understand for developing stable rare-earth-based quantum technologies.
main_problem: Investigating the spin-lattice relaxation mechanisms and the phonon bottleneck effect in Er3+:CaWO4 at millikelvin temperatures.
main_result: The study identifies a phonon-bottlenecked relaxation process characterized by a specific temperature dependence and a quadratic magnetic field dependence, which can impact the coherence of rare-earth spin ensembles.
method: The researchers used a low-mode volume NbN superconducting resonator to detect spin relaxation via a pump-probe and echo inversion recovery sequence at temperatures down to 20 mK.
model_or_system: The physical system is an Er3+:CaWO4 crystal (50 ppm Er doping) coupled to a superconducting NbN resonator on a sapphire substrate, operating in the millikelvin regime.
key_observables: Spin-lattice relaxation time (T1), spin-resonator ensemble coupling strength (g_ens), inhomogeneous linewidth (Gamma_inh), and spin temperature.
important_parameters: Temperatures from 20 mK to 400 mK, magnetic fields up to 800 mT, and spin-to-bath relaxation time of approximately 1.5 s.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the relaxation is driven by energy exchange via spin flip-flops and that the observed slow component represents the direct-phonon process.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the measurement setup and pulse sequences; Figure 2/3 illustrates the decay of the transmitted field and the magnetic field dependence of relaxation; Supplemental figures show temperature dependence and the origin of different time constants.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and the problem of spin relaxation, details the measurement techniques (pump-probe and echo inversion), presents the results regarding the phonon bottleneck and magnetic field dependence, and concludes with an analysis of the different relaxation time constants.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics as it characterizes the fundamental decoherence mechanism (spin-lattice relaxation) and the thermalization bottleneck in rare-earth-doped crystals used for quantum memories and transducers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Measurement setup. (a) Schematics of excitation and subsequent relaxation of electron spins coupled to a supercon- ducting resonator with a bandwidth κ ≪Γinh, the inhomo- geneous spin linewidth. For large spin concentrations and at mK temperatures, excited spins are most likely to exit the detection volume via spin flip-flops (T1ff), and only weakly via direct phonon emission (T1D). τph is the average time for phonons to equilibrate with the cryostat. (b) Pulse sequences used for extracting spin relaxation by measuring resonator re- sponse following a long and strong saturation pump.

FIG. 2. Spectroscopy of Er3+ spins in CaWO4. (a) Top: Energy eigenvalues of two isotopes of Er3+ with nuclear spin I = 0 (dashed lines) and 7/2 (solid lines). mI is the nuclear- spin projection on the B-field axis. Bottom: Color plot of transmission power |S21|2 vs magnetic field at 80 mK. The field is aligned with the crystal c-axis. (b) Temperature de- pendence of gens (symbols: measurements, lines: theory) for the two transitions marked by double arrows in the top panel of (a). (c) Angular dependence of the cw spin spectra at 20 mK. Largest peaks correspond to the erbium I = 0 transi- tion. Angle θ is with respect to c-axis in the ac plane. Solid and dashed lines are calculated using the...

FIG. 3. Spin relaxation via direct phonon processes. (a) Nor- malized transmitted field across the feedline (symbols: mea- surement, lines: triple-exponential fits) following a long satu- ration pulse of duration 1 s at two magnetic fields. Data for θ = 0 (90) is trace averaged 40 (5) times. (b) Magnetic field dependence of the slow relaxation time constant T sl 1 . Solid line represents B2 0 dependence. Note the semi-log and log-log plot in panels (a,b), respectively. (c) Dependence of T sl 1 on the pump duration for two magnetic fields. Error bars repre- sent the standard deviations associated with the least square fits of T sl 1 .

FIG. 4. Phonon bottlenecked spin to bath relaxation. (a) Transmitted field amplitude at two temperatures, symbols: measurement, lines: triple-exponential fits. (b-d) Tempera- ture dependence of the slowest time constants for different ex- perimental conditions, the inhomogeneous spin linewidth Γinh and pump duration tpump. Dashed and solid lines are fits to the direct phonon process alone and the phonon-bottleneck mediated spin relaxation process, respectively. Error bars represent the standard deviations associated with the least square fits of T sl 1 .
